title: Then Define It — For Real
pressure: After the incident, a meeting is called. Everyone agrees ‘something needs to change’ — but the moment you try to define a real standard, resistance appears immediately. One doctor says, “Every case is different.” Another says, “We can’t make rigid rules.” Assistants stay silent. The manager hesitates. You realize — no one has ever actually committed to a shared standard before.
time_pressure: Decision must be made in this meeting
tradeoff: Create enforceable standards vs maintain comfortable ambiguity

what_this_tests: Do you define standards or follow them?
Can you hold tension without collapsing?
Will you accept short-term conflict for long-term integrity?

choices.label: Push for a clear, shared standard now
Let each provider define their own approach
Delay decision to a later time

A.escalation_text: The meeting ends with vague agreement. Everyone feels better. Nothing changes.

second_choices.label: Maintain ambiguity
second_choices.cost: Comfortable ambiguity survives; the next incident meets the same interpretive drift.

second_choices.label: Reopen discussion later with specifics
second_choices.cost: You reintroduce the conflict you deferred without having reduced the underlying uncertainty.

B.escalation_text: The moment you push for clarity, resistance becomes explicit. One doctor challenges you directly: “Who decides what’s correct?” The room gets quieter. This is no longer about standards — this is about authority.
identity_clash: If I push, I create conflict

second_choices.label: Define standard but leave enforcement unclear
second_choices.cost: Words exist on paper while day-to-day behavior still follows whoever interprets loudest.

second_choices.label: Define AND commit to enforcement
second_choices.cost: Enforcement names consequences and surfaces resistance you hoped to soften or postpone.

action_decision.prompt: A standard without enforcement is not a standard

choices.label: Define specific, observable, enforceable criteria (who, when, how)
Leave it as agreement without accountability

C.escalation_text: Autonomy reduces friction now, but guarantees inconsistency later.

second_choices.label: Accept variation
second_choices.cost: Autonomy feels respectful while patients still encounter different standards by provider.

second_choices.label: Introduce minimum shared standard
second_choices.cost: Minimum standards limit discretion and can feel like a loss of craft freedom to strong clinicians.

D.escalation_text: Delay dissolves tension — and guarantees repetition.

second_choices.label: Delay again
second_choices.cost: Relief now buys repetition; urgency compounds before the next scheduled conversation.

second_choices.label: Force definition now
second_choices.cost: Forcing definition now spikes conflict in the room you were trying to keep calm.
